review_text:
My first 1 star review goes to...the very deserving Circus Circus.  This review has everything to do with their employees and nothing to do with the amenities of the hotel and casino.

We all know how fun it is to relive your childhood upstairs where all the games and circus shows go down so my friends and I spent some time up there.  We sat down to play Circus Downs, the game where you roll a ball into holes to try to get your horse across the board, but there was a cheater amongst us.  He stole a ball..and used 2 to play.  The worker did nothing to stop him and even awarded that jerk a prize at the end.  Seriously?! Little kids are playing this game and you're going to cheat them from a fair game??  When I asked the employee to let us play another round, he just ignored me!  He didn't even apologize for the cheater or simply say no.  A friend and I decided to report this situation to security...which turned out to be no help AT ALL.  

Us: *tap* Excuse me.
Security biatch: Don't touch me.
Us: I need to report an incident. We were upstairs-
Security: *with LOTS of attitude* Upstairs? Upstairs where? Midway? 
Us: Um...upstairs where all the gam..
Security: Get to the point. What.Do.You.Want?
*Oh hellll no! At this point..I've had it up to HERE with her attitude!*
Us: If you're busy and you need to be somewhere, then I can talk to someone else.  Just point me to the direction of a manager.
Security: *throws back her jacket flap to reveal her "badge* Security.
Us: Tells her the story...blah blah 
Security: What? So this is about a teddy bear? 
Us: NO, this about ethics and the way the employee inappropriately handled the situation.
Security: How old are you?
Us:......Why does that matter?
Security: I have the right to ask.

WHAT A BEEEEEEEEEEEEEEEEE!!! Really?! You're a security guard and you're going around riding on your high horse??  Are you even doing your job securing the premises? I wish I had gotten HER name so that I could report her!!

As you can guess, talking to the manager of midway was pointless.  He apologized for the "terrible predicament" we were in and how he understands "it sucks."  WHY ARE THEY TALKING TO US AS IF WE'RE 8 YEARS OLD?! 

adijgoiasjdgoiasdg CIRCUS CIRCUS YOU GUYS SUCK!!!
issue: Employee ignored cheating, allowed the cheater to win a prize, and failed to address the complaint or apologize
fixes: Deploy a real‑time incident‑reporting portal (e.g., ServiceNow Incident Management) with mandatory “Cheating” and “Complaint” categories; any report triggers an automatic escalation email to the floor manager within 15 minutes. | - Introduce a weekly compliance audit checklist in Power BI that pulls data from the POS system’s “Winner Verification” field, flagging any discrepancy > 0% for immediate corrective action by the shift lead. | - Create a scripted apology script in the CRM (Salesforce Service Cloud) that auto‑populates the case details and sends a pre‑approved apology template via SMS/WhatsApp within two business hours of a verified complaint. | - Launch a quarterly mystery‑shopper program using the Qualtrics CX suite, targeting scenarios where employees ignore cheating; scores below 90% generate a targeted coaching session delivered through the LMS (Cornerstone).
rationale: All recommendations directly address the customer’s issue of ignored cheating and failure to apologize. Each specifies tools (ServiceNow, Power BI, Salesforce, Qualtrics, Cornerstone), measurable actions, and clear triggers. The only deduction is in feasibility: implementing a real-time portal with mandatory categories and auto-escalation requires integration work, training, and system changes—moderate effort, not trivial. The use of POS data and SMS/WhatsApp automation also assumes existing infrastructure, which may not be in place.

review_text:
My review pertains only to the Pet Boarding facilities.

Their "Hound Hotel" sounds/looks really good online, but in person it was a real let down.  The kennel is in a room off the parking garage, steps from the city sidewalk.  I was told it was by a security desk, but when we arrived, that whole tower was closed and unattended.  If somone were able to get into the room, they could get to your dog through the space at the top of the cage, even though the cage was locked.  The cameras are a nice feature, but are you gonna watch the dog 24/7?  Even if you saw someone doing something with your dog, it would take you 10 minutes to get there from the other tower.  We packed up and went to the Reno Plaza hotel, who allows pets in the room.

If you care for your pet, you would not use these facilities.
issue: The online description of the "Hound Hotel" is misleading and does not match the actual facility experience
fixes: Deploy a weekly audit workflow in Confluence (or Trello) where the marketing team cross‑checks every product page against a standardized “Facility Checklist” template and logs any discrepancies; if mismatches are found, trigger an automated Slack alert to the content manager for immediate correction. | - Integrate a post‑stay SurveyMonkey NPS survey linked from the booking confirmation email; configure a rule in Zapier to auto‑populate any score ≤ 6 into a Jira ticket labeled “Description Misalignment,” ensuring a rapid fix within 48 hours. | - Use Google Analytics “Page Speed Insights” to identify pages with low load times (>2 s), then run A/B tests on the updated descriptions via Optimizely to verify improved clarity while measuring click‑through rates and conversion lift. | - Establish a quarterly review cycle in Asana where the product owner reviews all listing copy against verified property photos and video tours uploaded to the CMS, assigning a KPI of ≥90 % alignment before the next launch batch.
rationale: All recommendations directly address the core issue of mismatched online descriptions and actual facility experience. Each specifies concrete tools (Confluence, SurveyMonkey, Zapier, Jira, Optimizely, Asana, CMS) and measurable actions. Steps are clear and executable. However, feasibility is capped at 3 due to reliance on multiple integrations (Zapier, Optimizely), A/B testing infrastructure, and cross-team coordination—moderate effort requiring coordination beyond immediate execution.

issue: The boarding area is located in an unattended, closed tower, creating a security risk for pets
fixes: Deploy a cloud‑based access control solution (e.g., HID Go) with proximity cards and a dedicated “Pet Entry” door that logs every entry/exit event; integrate the log feed into a Power BI dashboard for real‑time monitoring. | - Install motion‑activated CCTV cameras linked to a remote monitoring station staffed by security guards during peak hours, and configure the system to send automatic alerts via Slack when the camera detects movement inside the tower after closing hours. | - Introduce a mobile pet check‑in app (using the existing Airline Mobile app) that requires users to upload a photo ID, vaccine certificate, and a timestamped confirmation before they can enter the tower, ensuring only authorized pets are present. | - Contract a local licensed security firm to provide on‑site patrols at least twice per hour, with patrol schedules recorded in a digital roster maintained in Asana and verified through weekly compliance reports.
rationale: Relevance is 5: All recommendations directly address the security risk of unattended access to the pet boarding tower. Concreteness is 5: Each recommendation specifies real tools (HID Go, Power BI, Slack, Airline Mobile app, Asana) and measurable actions. Actionability is 4: Steps are clear but require interpretation (e.g., 'integrate log feed' lacks exact API or data schema). Feasibility is 2: The proposal requires new hardware (CCTV, access control), third-party contracts (security firm), app development (check-in), and integration work—high effort, high cost, not trivial or low-effort. Not feasible for a budget-conscious COO.

review_text:
Ahhh Circus Circus Reno, lets take a tour shall we?  Keep in mind we're not expecting much, this IS Reno, not Vegas.

Lets check in!  Go to the front desk and be greeted by the most somber and unpleasant reception desk staff I've encountered.  Pretty much everybody we talk to up there seem to hate their life and made sure to show it.  What a great vibe to start our trip!

Take a walk through the casino floor on the way to the room.  Take a look around.  The wait staff looks like they've been working there since 1975, jaw-jacked clientele super focused on their slot game nearing the end of their 72 hour meth bender (bet your ass they're staying in the room next to you!).  Outstanding!  Unless you're waiting for the tram to take you to the Sky Tower (which "sometimes" works), looks like you're walking about 5-10 minutes to your room carrying all your shit, probably should have just got a bellhop, but expect the same bad customer service, so we'll do it ourselves.

Arriving in the room, you'll be greeted by questionable bedding and furniture that looks like it's been sitting in the room since about the time that the wait staff was hired (circa 1975 or so... at least the TV was from the late 80s or early 90s).  You might even be lucky enough to find some dirty towels in the bathroom.  If you forgot your lighter in the car you're shit outta luck, since there's no matches to be found in the room.

Spend about 60 minutes downstairs and get stared at by creepy crackheads and ignored by wait staff.  Lets go get a drink at the bar!  Why does the bartender act like I'm unconvincing him be even being there?  There's no "Hey bud what can I get cha" here, just a blank stare and a nod acknowledging that you did, in fact, place an order.

...


Word to the wise:  Go ahead and stay at any of the other Casinos apart from the Circus Circus-Silver Legacy-El Dorado group.  I'd recommend paying a little extra and staying at the Peppermill or Atlantis (Reno's best "Vegas-like" experience), or if you have to stay downtown, go ahead and stay at the Sands Regency, at least the staff is more friendly and the rooms are bigger, for a cheaper price.  Clientele is slightly on the shady side as well, but it's Reno, I wouldn't expect anything more.
issue: Reception and front‑desk staff were unfriendly and displayed a negative attitude
fixes: Deploy a weekly “Attitude Pulse Survey” via Qualtrics sent to all guests within 24 hours of checkout, automatically routing scores below 8/10 to the manager’s dashboard for immediate coaching. | - Implement a mandatory 15‑minute microlearning module on “Positive Greetings & Active Listening” in Lessonly, with a built‑in quiz that employees must pass before clocking in. | - Install video analytics (e.g., Axis Communications) at reception points to capture entryway greetings; set a KPI that ≥90 % of entries receive a smile and a welcome gesture within 2 weeks. | - Introduce a “Front Desk Buddy” program where every shift pairs a senior staff member with a new hire, tracked through a shared Google Sheet that logs weekly peer‑feedback meetings and a target improvement score of +20 % positive comments per month.
rationale: The recommendations are highly relevant, actionable, and concrete, naming specific tools (Qualtrics, Lessonly, Axis Communications, Google Sheets) and measurable KPIs. However, feasibility is rated 2 due to the high effort and cost of installing video analytics at reception points, which requires hardware, privacy compliance, and ongoing monitoring—making it impractical for immediate rollout. This single high-effort component drags down feasibility despite strong performance elsewhere.

These require significant capital investment, hardware deployment, and cross-team coordination—justifying a 2 on feasibility.

rationale: All recommendations are relevant, actionable, and concrete, naming specific tools (Kronos WFM, Oracle OPERA, Uber Freight, loyalty app). However, feasibility is scored 2 due to high effort: deploying RFID lockers, integrating third-party delivery via Uber Freight with SLA, and launching a gamified loyalty program require significant budget, vendor contracts, and system integration—none of which are trivial or low-effort. These are not 'existing tools' or 'today' rollouts, violating strict feasibility criteria.

You might even be lucky enough to find some dirty towels in the bathroom.  If you forgot your lighter in the car you're shit outta luck, since there's no matches to be found in the room.

Spend about 60 minutes downstairs and get stared at by creepy crackheads and ignored by wait staff.  Lets go get a drink at the bar!  Why does the bartender act like I'm unconvincing him be even being there?  There's no "Hey bud what can I get cha" here, just a blank stare and a nod acknowledging that you did, in fact, place an order.

...

Clientele is slightly on the shady side as well, but it's Reno, I wouldn't expect anything more.
issue: Room furnishings were outdated, bedding and towels were dirty, and basic amenities like matches were missing
fixes: Deploy a digital housekeeping checklist in iAuditor (SafetyCulture) that requires staff to scan QR codes on each bed linen, towel set, and amenity before completing a room, with automatic alerts if any item is marked “missing” or “dirty.” | - Install RFID‑enabled asset tags on all furniture, linens, and bathroom essentials; integrate the data into ServiceNow CMMS so that inventory levels are tracked in real time and replenishment orders are generated when stock falls below predefined thresholds. | - Schedule nightly deep‑cleaning audits via a mobile app such as HotSOS that records cleaning times per room, photos of completed tasks, and a KPI dashboard that flags rooms exceeding the 15‑minute cleanup window. | - Create a pre‑arrival “Essential Amenities” kit stocked with branded items (matches, toothbrushes, etc.) and track usage through barcode scanning; order replacement kits automatically from the hotel’s ERP (e.g., Oracle Hospitality) when kit depletion triggers a low‑stock alert.
rationale: All recommendations directly address room cleanliness issues with specific tools (iAuditor, RFID, HotSOS, ERP) and measurable actions. Each is actionable with clear steps and concrete metrics. However, feasibility is capped at 3 due to the high effort required: RFID tagging, system integration with ServiceNow CMMS, and ERP automation are not trivial, requiring significant capital investment, cross-system integration, and vendor coordination—far beyond 'existing tools' or 'today' implementation.

fixes: Deploy AI‑driven video analytics (e.g., Verkada’s Smart Alert) on all entry points to detect loitering patterns and send real‑time SMS alerts to security staff when suspicious activity exceeds predefined thresholds. | - Install RFID access gates integrated with the existing Property Management System (PMS) so only verified guests receive entry codes; log every gate opening and review daily usage reports to flag unauthorized entries. | - Contract a licensed security firm to conduct random patrols using GPS‑tracked mobile apps (e.g., PatrolBase) and require weekly compliance reports detailing number of visits, duration, and any incidents recorded. | - Launch a “SafeStay” program that offers complimentary on‑site drug‑testing kits (e.g., Rapid Drug Screen test cards) and a QR‑code check‑in system linked to the PMS to record guest participation rates and reduce repeat offenders. | - Partner with local law enforcement to establish a quarterly community safety audit, documenting crime statistics and creating a joint action plan to address identified hot spots within 48 hours of the audit completion.
rationale: All recommendations are highly relevant to the safety theme and directly address the customer’s concern about drug-using individuals. However, feasibility is rated 2 due to high cost and complexity: contracting a licensed security firm, deploying AI video analytics, distributing drug-testing kits, and coordinating with law enforcement for audits are all high-effort, expensive, and risky initiatives that exceed the threshold for 'low effort' or 'trivial' implementation.

review_text: Raquel, Brittney, & some middle eastern dude that works in the VIP downstairs....all terrible customer service. As a VIP member I should be receiving better care. They were all rude and unhelpful. I will never stay here again. I can not believe this service. It takes over an hour after talking to 4 different people and I still don't have a wifi connection after I paid for it? Not okay.
issue: Rude and unhelpful staff providing poor customer service, especially for VIP members
fixes: Deploy a real‑time sentiment monitoring dashboard in Zendesk Explore that flags any ticket tagged “VIP” with a CSAT score < 3, automatically routing it to a senior supervisor for immediate follow‑up within 2 hours. | - Institute a mandatory quarterly 90‑minute “VIP Service Excellence” workshop using the TalentLMS LMS, where agents complete scenario‑based simulations scored against a rubric and receive personalized feedback via the platform’s built‑in analytics. | - Introduce a weekly “VIP‑First Call Resolution” KPI tracked in Salesforce Service Cloud, setting a target of ≥95% first‑call resolution for VIP inquiries and triggering a corrective action workflow if the threshold is missed for two consecutive weeks. | - Implement a post‑interaction survey (via Qualtrics) that sends a one‑click NPS rating link to all VIP customers; integrate the results into Power BI to generate a monthly report on “VIP NPS trend” and trigger coaching alerts when scores fall below 7.
rationale: All recommendations directly address the theme of poor service quality for VIPs with precise tools and measurable actions. Each is actionable and concrete, naming specific systems (Zendesk, TalentLMS, Salesforce, Qualtrics, Power BI). However, feasibility is capped at 3 due to reliance on cross-system integrations (e.g., Qualtrics to Power BI, Salesforce workflow triggers) and mandatory training rollouts, which require coordination, configuration, and likely budget—moderate effort beyond existing tools.

review_text:
I work in Reno Feb-March, for the last ....years, beds have not been upgraded for many years, the air quality is very bad, especially for one whose profession is a vocalist.  haven't tried the food here,  my room had a broken heater and had to be replaced.  All in all, Would never stay here, but it's provided by my employer, so that's the catch.  don't know how Reno will sustain these large hotel casinos with such poor entertainment.

I'm on the road about 6 months a year throughout North America and Europe, and about 95% of hotels have free wifi.  Circus Circus charges $10.00 per day for wifi, an added out of pocket expense for business travellers.  For one who must make many international calls, free wifi is a must for making travel reservations.  The only hotels charging for wifi these days are the big budget hotels, or downtown Reno.  Just Sayin', time to change Circus Circus.   Not happy paying for premium wireless, when I can't log on to internet banking or try to watch a news video of a dangerous high wind weather alert.  Just a note to all of you who like comfortable new beds,  I would go with El Dorado.  They have new flat screen TVs, but Circus Circus still has the 1990 model.  You'd think with all of that gambling revenue, they could upgrade some important things.  Hotel premium wireless is $13.00 per day,  download speeds are .31 MBPS,  You might be able to watch a 30 second video in 3 hours at that rate.  Unacceptable.  
On a positive note, some good live music in the Lounge.
issue: Beds are outdated and uncomfortable, not upgraded for years.
fixes: Deploy a quarterly “Bed Refresh” KPI in your PMS (e.g., Opera) with a target upgrade rate of ≥20 % per quarter; trigger automatic purchase orders when the stock‑level report shows beds older than three years. | - Install IoT pressure‑sensing mattresses linked to a cloud dashboard (such as Sensitech’s SleepSense) to monitor nightly usage and flag any bed that exceeds the comfort threshold, prompting immediate replacement. | - Conduct a vendor audit using a standardized RFP template in Coupa that scores mattress quality, warranty terms, and delivery SLAs, then lock in the top two suppliers for a 12‑month contract with built‑in cost‑saving clauses. | - Launch a pilot of modular, height‑adjustable frames (e.g., FlexSleep™) on five floors, tracking guest satisfaction via Qualtrics NPS surveys and rolling out full‑scale rollout if the pilot meets a 15 % improvement in bed‑comfort scores.
rationale: All recommendations directly address outdated bed comfort with specific tools (Opera, Sensitech’s SleepSense, Coupa, Qualtrics) and measurable actions. Each is actionable with clear steps and concrete metrics. Feasibility is capped at 3 due to the high effort required for IoT deployment, pilot rollout, and vendor contract lock-in, which exceeds low-effort thresholds. No generic advice; all elements are grounded in real systems and metrics.

issue: Wi‑Fi costs extra, is slow (~0.31 Mbps), and inadequate for business needs.
fixes: Deploy enterprise-grade Wi‑Fi hardware (e.g., Cisco Meraki MR‑54) with 802.11ac Wave 2 capability, then configure QoS policies in the controller to prioritize business traffic over guest bandwidth, guaranteeing at least 100 Mbps per client. | - Install a managed SD‑WAN solution such as VMware SD‑WAN by VeloCloud, enabling automatic failover between primary ISP links and cloud‑hosted VPN tunnels, and set a minimum link speed threshold of 150 Mbps for all business applications. | - Use a network performance monitoring tool like SolarWinds NPM to continuously measure WAN latency (<15 ms) and throughput (>250 Mbps) for key business services, triggering alerts when metrics fall below thresholds and prompting immediate remediation. | - Offer a bundled “Business Premium” Wi‑Fi package that includes unlimited high‑speed data, QoS management, and a dedicated account manager; price it at $25/month and automate activation via the existing billing portal (e.g., Stripe Billing).
rationale: While all recommendations are highly relevant, actionable, and concrete, feasibility is rated 1 because deploying enterprise-grade hardware, SD-WAN, and network monitoring tools requires significant capital expenditure, vendor contracts, and infrastructure changes—far beyond 'trivial' or 'low effort.' The bundled service is also unrealistic: charging $25/month for a premium Wi-Fi package that delivers 100 Mbps per client contradicts the customer’s reported speed of 0.31 Mbps, suggesting systemic network failure, not a pricing model fix. This response ignores the core issue: the existing network is fundamentally broken, and these solutions assume a functional baseline.

review_text: My fiancé and I will no longer be valued customers as we were not treat with respect. Worst experience ever!!!
issue: Staff were disrespectful and did not treat us with respect
fixes: Deploy a real‑time sentiment monitoring dashboard in Qualtrics (or Medallia) linked to staff performance scores so supervisors receive instant alerts when a negative “disrespect” rating is logged; trigger automatic coaching sessions within 24 hours via LMS modules on the company’s Cornerstone OnDemand portal. | - Institute mandatory microlearning modules on respectful customer interaction delivered through the Lessonly platform, with weekly quiz scores tracked and tied to quarterly bonus eligibility. | - Launch a peer‑review feedback loop in the Officevibe pulse survey app where every employee rates their colleagues’ respectfulness on a 10‑point scale; managers review the top‑scoring employees quarterly and award recognition certificates to reinforce positive behavior. | - Introduce a “Rude‑Response” incident tracking ticketing system in Zendesk with a KPI that only 0.5% of tickets per month should contain disrespect tags, automatically routing those cases to the Customer Experience Manager for immediate remediation.
rationale: All recommendations directly address disrespect in customer service with specific tools and measurable actions. Each is actionable and concrete, naming real platforms and KPIs. However, feasibility is rated 2 because deploying real-time sentiment monitoring, mandatory microlearning tied to bonuses, peer-review feedback loops, and a new incident tracking system in Zendesk all require significant integration, training, and budget—far beyond trivial or low effort. These are high-effort, multi-department initiatives that cannot be rolled out today without major investment.

review_text:
I give it 2 stars only because it was so darn cheap.  I stayed there for 30 a night. I mean c'mon.  It really doesn't get cheaper than that - even regular motels are more expensive. 

The games there aren't what they used to be when I went as a child and they're so much more pricey.  It's fun to play them again though and win those useless prizes. I actually had fun playing the arcade games where you win tickets and trade them in though.  They still have the old school games that I don't even remember the names of because they're that old.   The shows were so half assed but I wasn't expecting an extravagant show anyway.  It's sad because reno is so empty unless there's an event going on.  The food in that vicinity wasn't that great either and I noticed while and after eating that everyone that was there was 60+.  It's a great meat market for the seniors! haha
issue: The motel is extremely cheap, suggesting low quality and poor value.
fixes: Deploy a quarterly price‑benchmarking dashboard in Pricefx that compares your nightly rates against local competitors; adjust pricing within 10 % of the average competitor’s mid‑range rate when the gap exceeds 15 %. | - Introduce a tiered room‑upgrade package (e.g., “Standard Plus” with complimentary breakfast) priced at $10–$20 more than the base rate, tracked via the property management system’s “Room Configuration” module so staff automatically upsell eligible guests. | - Launch a post‑stay survey through Qualtrics that asks guests to rate value on a 1‑5 scale; trigger a targeted email offer (e.g., 10 % discount coupon) for any rating ≤3, measured by a 30 % increase in repeat bookings over three months. | - Add a QR‑code‑linked guest experience feedback kiosk in the lobby that records real‑time satisfaction scores; set a KPI that ≥85 % of scores must exceed 4/5 before the marketing team creates a promotional video highlighting upgraded amenities and premium services.
rationale: All recommendations directly address the pricing-value perception issue by linking pricing strategy, product differentiation, and feedback loops to guest perception. Each is specific, names real tools (Pricefx, Qualtrics, PMS), and includes measurable actions. Feasibility is penalized because deploying Pricefx, a specialized pricing tool, requires integration and likely cost, and the QR kiosk with real-time KPIs implies new hardware and software setup—neither trivial. Thus, feasibility is 3, not 5.

The games there aren't what they used to be when I went as a child and they're so much more pricey.  It's fun to play them again though and win those useless prizes. I actually had fun playing the arcade games where you win tickets and trade them in though.  They still have the old school games that I don't even remember the names of because they're that old.   The shows were so half assed but I wasn't expecting an extravagant show anyway.  It's sad because reno is so empty unless there's an event going on.  The food in that vicinity wasn't that great either and I noticed while and after eating that everyone that was there was 60+.  It's a great meat market for the seniors! haha
issue: Arcade games are outdated and overpriced, diminishing enjoyment.
fixes: Deploy a quarterly “Game Refresh” schedule in Asana that sources new titles from a curated library (e.g., Funwhip) and requires approval by the Entertainment Manager within two weeks, with budget tracked via the expense module. | - Install QR‑code linked price‑comparison widgets on every arcade cabinet using Shopify’s POS API so customers instantly see local competitor rates before purchase, then monitor conversion lift weekly in Google Analytics. | - Add a tiered loyalty program integrated with Stripe Billing that awards free credits after five game plays and automatically emails a personalized discount coupon when credit balance falls below $10. | - Set up real‑time player analytics through PlayOnCloud’s cloud dashboard to identify under‑utilized machines; redeploy those units with the top‑performing titles identified in the previous month’s usage report.
rationale: All recommendations are highly relevant, actionable, and concrete, naming specific tools (Asana, Shopify POS API, Stripe Billing, PlayOnCloud) and measurable actions. However, feasibility is rated 2 due to high effort: integrating Shopify POS API with arcade cabinets, deploying QR-code widgets across multiple machines, and building a tiered loyalty program with automated email triggers require significant technical integration, cross-team coordination, and likely new development work—far beyond trivial or low-effort changes.

issue: Live shows are half-assed and lack quality.
fixes: Deploy a standardized performance‑audit checklist in Google Forms that every show manager completes after each run; set a threshold score (e.g., ≥ 85%) and automatically flag any act scoring below it for immediate corrective action. | - Use a video analytics platform such as Vidyard to record live streams and generate a weekly “Quality Score” report, targeting a minimum of 90% audience engagement metrics per show segment. | - Institute a rotating creative team schedule in Asana that assigns two seasoned producers and one up‑and‑coming artist per week, with KPIs tracked via Asana’s built‑in time‑tracking feature. | - Launch a quarterly talent‑assessment workshop using the TalentLMS LMS, where performers receive scored rubrics on presentation, vocal delivery, and stage presence, and results feed into a promotion matrix tied to the next season’s lineup.
rationale: Relevance is 5: All recommendations directly address live show quality. Concreteness is 5: Specific tools (Google Forms, Vidyard, Asana, TalentLMS) and metrics (85%, 90% engagement, time-tracking) are named. Actionability is 4: Steps are clear but require interpretation (e.g., 'flag for corrective action' lacks process). Feasibility is 2: Launching a quarterly workshop with a new LMS, video analytics, and mandatory post-show audits across all shows is high effort, expensive, and not trivial to implement—especially with no mention of existing infrastructure. No existing tools are leveraged; all require new setup.

issue: Food in the vicinity is not good, offering poor taste and quality.
fixes: Deploy a digital menu‑audit checklist in iAuditor (SafetyCulture) with photo evidence; require kitchen staff to complete it after each service, and automatically flag any item scoring below 8/10 for immediate review. | - Launch a quarterly “Taste Panel” where a rotating group of 12 guests rates dishes on a standardized Google Form (e.g., rating scale = 0‑5); feed the aggregated scores into a Power BI dashboard and trigger corrective actions when average ratings fall below 3.5. | - Introduce a supplier scorecard in SAP Ariba that tracks freshness metrics (days since harvest), ingredient sourcing origin, and price variance, and set a minimum 90% compliance threshold for continued business. | - Install IoT temperature sensors linked to the existing kitchen management system (e.g., KitchenCUT) that send real‑time alerts if food holding temps exceed 45 °F for more than 20 minutes, allowing immediate recipe adjustments or batch disposal.
rationale: All recommendations directly address food quality with specific tools and measurable actions. Each is actionable and concrete, naming real systems (iAuditor, SAP Ariba, Power BI, KitchenCUT) and exact metrics. Feasibility is 3 because while tools exist, integrating IoT sensors with KitchenCUT and implementing supplier scorecards in SAP Ariba likely require cross-department coordination, new workflows, and data integration—moderate effort beyond existing systems.

issue: The venue primarily attracts seniors, making the atmosphere less appealing to younger guests.
fixes: Deploy a “Young‑Adult Targeted Marketing” campaign through Instagram Stories (using Canva templates) and TikTok ads with a 30 % discount coupon code, tracking conversions via Google Analytics UTM tags to ensure at least 200 new bookings from ages 18‑35 within two months. | - Install digital wayfinding kiosks powered by the VenueNext app that display real‑time event schedules and filter options (“Age Range,” “Music Genre”), then monitor usage metrics weekly to keep average time spent on site >8 minutes per guest. | - Partner with local colleges/young professional groups to host monthly themed events (e.g., trivia nights, live music jams) and measure attendance growth against baseline senior‑only events by logging RSVPs in Eventbrite’s “Attendee Demographics” dashboard. | - Create a dynamic age‑segmented playlist on Spotify for Business that automatically switches between older and contemporary tracks based on a sensor‑driven “age detection” API, and track listener retention rates in the platform’s analytics to maintain a minimum NPS score of 70 among Gen Z/Gen Y users.
rationale: Relevance is 5: all recommendations directly address the atmosphere issue by targeting younger guests. Concreteness is 5: each uses specific tools (Canva, TikTok, VenueNext, Eventbrite, Spotify for Business) and measurable KPIs. Actionability is 4: steps are clear but require interpretation (e.g., 'age detection API' is vague). Feasibility is 2: the 'sensor-driven age detection API' is unproven, expensive, and likely violates privacy laws; deploying it across a venue is high effort and risky. This single flawed recommendation undermines feasibility.

review_text: One of the waiters she was old ugly and rude bad service went was playing at the machine
issue: The waiter was rude and impolite toward the customer.
fixes: Deploy a real‑time guest feedback kiosk (e.g., GuestPulse) at each table that triggers an instant SMS alert to the floor manager if a rating < 4 is logged; the manager must respond within 15 minutes with corrective action. | - Integrate a microlearning module on “Dining Etiquette” into the staff LMS (e.g., Cornerstone OnDemand), requiring a 90% pass score on the embedded quiz before a server’s next shift. | - Install wearable employee monitoring devices such as the Kronos Workforce Central wristband to log verbal tone and interaction duration; set a weekly compliance threshold (e.g., ≥95% positive sentiment per shift) with automatic coaching notifications in the HRIS. | - Schedule a monthly 20‑minute “Mystery Diner” audit where a trained observer records service quality scores on a standardized checklist; tie results to individual performance bonuses via the payroll integration (e.g., ADP). | - Launch a quarterly “Rude‑Free Zone” certification program using a scenario‑based e‑learning course hosted on TalentLMS, mandating a minimum of three consecutive days without any negative incident reports from the mobile feedback app.
rationale: Relevance is reduced due to overreach: the customer issue is a single rude waiter, but recommendations target systemic, organization-wide surveillance and incentives. Actionability fails due to vague implementation steps (e.g., 'set a threshold' without specifying how). Concreteness is undermined by unrealistic tools like 'Kronos Workforce Central wristband' for tone detection. Feasibility is 1: wearable monitoring devices are invasive, costly, and legally risky; certification programs and mystery diners require extensive coordination. These are not trivial or low-effort fixes.

Concreteness is strong with specific tools (Toast, Deputy, Lessonly, Qualtrics). Feasibility is low (2): implementing real-time dashboards, mystery guests with KPI-linked bonuses, and mandatory quizzes across staff requires significant investment, training, and integration—far beyond 'trivial' or 'low effort.'

review_text:
We made reservations just a day prior and then arrive only to be told that all circus performance acts are off until they finish construction of a new stage (which is due to open in Nov). This issue here is they made no mention of this on their webpage. When I complained they offered a full refund on the spot, but after driving all day we were not in the mood to shop around for another hotel.
Also, it was apparent the bedding either wasn't washed or not washed enough.
issue: Circus performance was cancelled without prior notice on the website, leading to disappointment.
fixes: Deploy an automated cancellation notification workflow in Salesforce Service Cloud that triggers a personalized email (via Mailchimp) and SMS (via Twilio) within 10 minutes of any show being canceled, ensuring guests receive immediate confirmation before arriving. | - Integrate a real‑time event status API into the public website (e.g., using Zapier to pull data from the ticketing system) so that upcoming shows are automatically marked “Cancelled” if the venue is unavailable, preventing false advertising. | - Create a dedicated “Cancellation Policy” page linked from every ticket purchase flow; configure it with live countdown timers (built with JavaScript) that display the exact time until the next scheduled update, reducing uncertainty. | - Set up a post‑event survey via Qualtrics that includes a mandatory “Was your cancellation communicated?” question, feeding results daily into a Power BI dashboard to monitor compliance rates and drive continuous improvement.
rationale: All recommendations directly address the core issue of unannounced cancellations and poor communication. Each is highly actionable with clear tools and steps. Concrete tools (Salesforce, Mailchimp, Twilio, Zapier, Qualtrics, Power BI, JavaScript) are named explicitly. Feasibility is rated 3 because while the tools are real, integrating multiple systems (Zapier, Twilio, Mailchimp, Salesforce) across event status and ticketing requires cross-department coordination, API access, and configuration—moderate effort beyond trivial or low-cost setup.

review_text:
This review is on the kids Midway game area:

As a child, I lived for Circus Circus! My family would come here several times a year and it was amazing and fun.

I took my 9 year old son here this past weekend for the first time and it was garbage to how it was before. They did not at all upgrade this place. They even took out one of the popular games Fantasia! The shows they had going on werent that entertaining. The games were getting overpriced and there wasn't really any new and fun exciting games. My son basically got bored in an hour and really wasn't that entertained by everything. I had to agree with him big time because I knew it was trash compared to what it was before. They need to do a huge upgrade to this place! I'm sure if they did, they would get a lot more visitors.
issue: The kids Midway area has not been upgraded and feels outdated compared to its past condition
fixes: Conduct a rapid‑fire redesign sprint using Miro’s “Design Sprint” template, assigning a cross‑functional team (architecture, operations, marketing) to sketch 3–5 concept layouts within 48 hours and present them to stakeholders for a vote. | - Deploy a digital signage network (e.g., Samsung Smart Signage) in the kids’ zone to display interactive games and rotating content, updating it weekly via the integrated CMS and tracking engagement metrics through Google Analytics. | - Install motion‑activated LED lighting (such as Philips Hue Play) on key attraction structures to create dynamic visual effects, controlled via the Philips Hue API so brightness levels are optimized based on foot‑traffic data from the venue’s POS system. | - Launch a mobile app gamification campaign using Unity’s real‑time multiplayer SDK, allowing children to earn virtual badges and redeem in‑zone prizes, with redemption rates monitored daily in the App Store Connect dashboard.
rationale: Relevance is moderate: the recommendations address modernization but miss the core issue—lack of upgrades despite past condition. Actionability is limited: steps like 'assigning a team' or 'voting' are vague. Concreteness is strong with specific tools (Miro, Philips Hue, Unity). Feasibility is 1: deploying digital signage, motion-activated lighting, and a mobile app gamification campaign across a kids' zone is financially and operationally ruinous without prior investment, especially for a venue likely not already equipped with POS-integrated analytics or CMS infrastructure.

issue: A popular game, Fantasia, was removed from the venue
fixes: Deploy a real‑time analytics dashboard (e.g., Power BI) linked to the ticketing system that triggers an automatic alert when any title’s weekly play count falls below a preset threshold (e.g., 150 plays per week), prompting immediate re‑evaluation before removal. | - Launch a quarterly “Games Recovery” sprint using Jira Kanban boards where the product team assigns owners, creates milestones (research new titles, negotiate licensing deals, set up beta testing), and tracks progress with velocity charts. | - Establish a vendor scorecard in a shared Google Sheet that scores suppliers on content diversity, revenue contribution, and availability; require a minimum 90% compliance rating before renewing contracts for games like Fantasia. | - Introduce a rapid‑prototype pilot program that lets guests vote via QR codes on lobby screens; if a target number of votes (say, ≥200) is reached within two weeks, the game automatically gets added back into rotation through an automated workflow in ServiceNow.
rationale: All recommendations are relevant to restoring Fantasia and preventing future removals. Each is actionable with clear steps and concrete tools (Power BI, Jira, Google Sheet, ServiceNow). Concreteness is strong with specific thresholds, tools, and metrics. However, feasibility is low: the rapid-prototype pilot requires new QR code infrastructure, guest-facing lobby screens, and automated workflows in ServiceNow—none of which are guaranteed to exist. The vendor scorecard also assumes pre-existing contract renewal processes. These are high-effort, non-trivial changes, justifying a 2 on feasibility.

rationale: Relevance is 5: All recommendations directly target entertainment quality via measurable engagement. Actionability is 4: Steps are clear but assume access to tools without confirming integration feasibility. Feasibility is 2: AI curation (Watson), real-time dashboards, and gamified app deployment require significant integration, training, and budget—high effort, not trivial or low-cost. Not feasible for a budget-conscious COO.

issue: The games are priced too high, making them feel overpriced
fixes: Deploy a price‑elasticity analysis in Google Data Studio (or Tableau) to compare sales volume versus price points; adjust individual game prices by up to 10% on identified “price‑sensitive” titles within two weeks. | - Launch a limited‑time “Flash Sale” series using Shopify’s Discount Code API, targeting a 20% discount on all games during off‑peak hours and tracking conversion uplift via Mixpanel events. | - Introduce a tiered pricing bundle in App Store Connect (e.g., Basic, Premium, Ultimate), then monitor churn rates through Firebase Analytics to ensure at least a 15% reduction in perceived overpricing while maintaining average order value. | - Implement a post‑purchase NPS survey in Qualtrics with a dedicated “Value for Price Paid” question; route any score ≤ 6 responses to a Zapier workflow that triggers a personalized refund offer or upgrade incentive within 24 hours.
rationale: All recommendations directly address the theme of perceived overpricing with specific, measurable actions. Each includes concrete tools (Google Data Studio, Shopify API, App Store Connect, Firebase, Qualtrics, Zapier) and clear metrics. Actions are highly actionable with explicit steps. Feasibility is reduced to 3 because implementing tiered bundles in App Store Connect and integrating Zapier workflows with real-time triggers requires cross-team coordination, configuration, and potential approval delays—moderate effort beyond immediate rollout.

fixes: Deploy a quarterly “Game Discovery Sprint” using Trello boards to source, test, and launch at least two new titles sourced from the Steam Early Access pipeline; track adoption with PlayFab analytics and aim for a ≥10% increase in active players per month. | - Introduce a “Kids‑Only Content Hub” on the website built with WordPress + Elementor, featuring curated weekly game trailers, user‑generated reviews, and a “Top‑Played This Week” leaderboard to drive repeat visits and keep kids engaged. | - Launch a “Gamification Challenge” via Strive Gamified LMS where kids earn points by completing short, interactive tutorials on the platform’s existing games, automatically awarding badges and unlocking exclusive mini‑games after reaching predefined milestones. | - Create a “Game Feedback Loop” using SurveyMonkey NPS surveys sent to parents after every update, triggering a Slack alert to the product team when satisfaction falls below 70%, enabling rapid iteration cycles.
rationale: All recommendations are highly relevant, actionable, and concrete, naming specific tools (Trello, PlayFab, WordPress, Strive, SurveyMonkey, Slack).
